Title: Host Pah1p phosphatidate phosphatase limits viral replication by regulating phospholipid synthesis

Common_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Major membrane structural lipids required for membrane biogenesis and support of viral replication complexes
Common_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: PAH1 represses phospholipid accumulation and thereby limits BMV replication; deletion increases total phospholipids and enhances BMV replication, whereas overexpression inhibits replication
Common_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. Deletion of PAH1 increased BMV positive- and negative-strand RNA accumulation by about 3-fold in yeast. 2. pah1Δ mutants showed increased total phospholipids, PA, ErgE, and free fatty acids with reduced DAG and TAG during BMV replication. 3. pah1Δ dgk1Δ retained enhanced BMV replication despite normal nuclear membrane shape, indicating altered lipid composition rather than membrane expansion is the major cause. 4. PAH1 deletion improved host cell growth during BMV replication. 5. Overexpression of yeast PAH1 inhibited BMV replication.
Common_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Gene deletion mutant analysis; overexpression in yeast; Northern blotting of BMV RNAs; lipid radiolabeling/TLC profiling; TEM and immunofluorescence localization; growth assays
Common_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Deletion promotes viral replication despite improving host growth during infection; shifts lipid flux away from TAG toward phospholipids, ErgE, and free fatty acids
Common_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Manipulating PAH1/LIPIN-like activity can tune phospholipid versus storage lipid allocation, but reduced PAH1 activity may increase susceptibility to (+)RNA virus replication; overexpression may be useful for antiviral engineering
Common_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Core causal effect is on host lipid homeostasis rather than direct antiviral signaling; paper shows PAH1 is a limiting host factor for viral replication by diverting PA toward storage lipid synthesis instead of phospholipid synthesis

Common_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Major membrane lipids supporting membrane biogenesis and viral replication complexes
Common_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: NEM1 deletion enhances BMV replication through inactivation of Pah1p-dependent lipid control; overexpression inhibits BMV replication
Common_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. nem1Δ increased BMV RNA replication approximately 2-fold. 2. NEM1 acts with SPO7 to activate Pah1p. 3. Overexpression of NEM1 inhibited BMV replication similarly to PAH1 overexpression.
Common_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Deletion mutant analysis; overexpression; Northern blotting
Common_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Loss of NEM1 favors viral replication by disrupting host lipid regulation
Common_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Not a direct crop engineering target here, but supports the concept that PAP activation modules can be leveraged for antiviral lipid-state engineering
Common_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Included as a core host factor because it was directly tested in Results as a required activator of Pah1p affecting the metabolite state and viral phenotype

Common_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Major membrane lipids supporting membrane biogenesis and viral replication complexes
Common_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: SPO7 deletion enhances BMV replication through inactivation of Pah1p-dependent lipid control; overexpression inhibits BMV replication
Common_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. spo7Δ increased BMV RNA replication approximately 2-fold. 2. SPO7 acts with NEM1 to activate Pah1p. 3. Overexpression of SPO7 inhibited BMV replication similarly to PAH1 overexpression.
Common_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Deletion mutant analysis; overexpression; Northern blotting
Common_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Loss of SPO7 favors viral replication by disrupting host lipid regulation
Common_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Supports PAP-activating modules as potential antiviral metabolic engineering targets
Common_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Included because direct results linked SPO7 perturbation to altered lipid-control state and replication phenotype

Pathway_Genes.Pathway_Branch_or_Subpathway: PA-DAG interconversion controlling phospholipid versus TAG synthesis
Pathway_Genes.End_Product_Connection_Type: Supplies precursor pool for phospholipid synthesis and diverts flux away from TAG toward phospholipids
Pathway_Genes.Rate_Limiting_or_Key_Control_Step: Key control step for lipid flux balance in this study
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Major membrane lipids required for membrane formation and viral replication complex biogenesis
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: DGK1 overexpression enhanced BMV replication to pah1Δ-like levels; catalytic activity was required
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. Overexpression of DGK1 increased BMV replication to levels comparable with pah1Δ cells. 2. Catalytically inactive D177A mutant failed to enhance BMV replication. 3. Results support that increasing PA and redirecting lipid synthesis from TAG to phospholipids promotes BMV replication.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Overexpression; catalytic mutant comparison (D177A); Northern blotting
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Increasing DGK1 activity may favor viral replication by elevating PA/phospholipid synthesis at expense of storage lipid synthesis
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: A cautionary target: boosting DGK/LPA precursor supply can increase membrane lipid pools that may benefit (+)RNA virus replication
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: The study uses DGK1 both as a causal test of PA increase and in pah1Δ dgk1Δ genetics to dissociate membrane expansion from lipid composition

Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Catalyzes methylation of phosphatidylethanolamine toward phosphatidylcholine synthesis
Pathway_Genes.End_Product_Connection_Type: Directly supports synthesis of the major membrane phospholipid required for viral replication
Pathway_Genes.Rate_Limiting_or_Key_Control_Step: Important control point for PC supply in this study
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Major membrane phospholipid supporting replication-site membrane biogenesis
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: CHO2 is required for the strongly enhanced BMV replication in pah1Δ cells, and CHO2 overexpression promotes BMV replication in wild type
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. cho2Δ nearly abolished BMV replication in the RS453 background. 2. In pah1Δ cho2Δ cells, the enhanced BMV replication seen in pah1Δ mutants was strongly diminished. 3. Overexpression of CHO2 increased BMV RNA accumulation in wild-type cells. 4. Active PC synthesis is required for the phospholipid-mediated enhancement of BMV replication.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Deletion mutant analysis; overexpression from endogenous or GAL1 promoter; Northern blotting; Western blotting of Cho2p-HA
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Enhanced PC synthesis can favor viral replication; cho2 deletion suppresses replication but may impair normal membrane lipid homeostasis
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Manipulating PC biosynthesis can modulate virus-supportive membrane environments; reduced CHO2-like activity could have antiviral value but may compromise membrane function
Pathway_Genes.Interacting_Proteins: Opi1p (transcriptional context reported for phospholipid synthesis genes)
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Paper suggests PC is important but not solely sufficient to explain the full pah1Δ phenotype, implying contribution from total phospholipid increase beyond PC alone

Pathway_Genes.Pathway_Branch_or_Subpathway: PA-to-DAG conversion controlling TAG versus phospholipid synthesis
Pathway_Genes.End_Product_Connection_Type: Competes with phospholipid synthesis by consuming PA and channeling flux toward storage lipid synthesis
Pathway_Genes.Rate_Limiting_or_Key_Control_Step: Key control step for lipid flux allocation
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Membrane structural lipids supporting viral replication complex formation
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Heterologous expression of AtPAH1 inhibited BMV replication in yeast
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. AtPAH1 is a plant ortholog of yeast PAH1. 2. Expression of AtPAH1 in yeast inhibited BMV replication by approximately 40–50%.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Heterologous expression in yeast; Northern blotting
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Likely lowers phospholipid availability for viral replication but may redirect lipids toward storage
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Supports PAP overexpression as a cross-kingdom antiviral metabolic engineering strategy
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Included because directly assayed as a functional ortholog affecting phospholipid-linked replication phenotype in applied yeast system

Pathway_Genes.Applied_Promoters: GAL1 promoter in yeast; enhanced CaMV 35S promoter in N. benthamiana
Pathway_Genes.Pathway_Branch_or_Subpathway: PA-to-DAG conversion controlling TAG versus phospholipid synthesis
Pathway_Genes.End_Product_Connection_Type: Competes with phospholipid synthesis by consuming PA and channeling flux toward storage lipid synthesis
Pathway_Genes.Rate_Limiting_or_Key_Control_Step: Key control step for lipid flux allocation
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Membrane structural lipids supporting viral replication complex formation
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Expression of AtPAH2 inhibited BMV replication in yeast and in N. benthamiana; it also inhibited TMV replication in N. benthamiana
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. AtPAH2 expression in yeast inhibited BMV replication by approximately 40–50%. 2. Transient expression of AtPAH2 in N. benthamiana inhibited BMV positive-strand RNA3 accumulation by about 40–50%. 3. AtPAH2 also inhibited TMV replication by about 50%, but did not significantly reduce TRV genomic RNAs 1 and 2.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Heterologous expression in yeast; transient overexpression in N. benthamiana by agroinfiltration; Northern blotting of viral RNAs
Pathway_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Agroinfiltration-mediated overexpression followed by BMV/TMV/TRV infection in N. benthamiana; galactose-induced expression in yeast
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Likely decreases phospholipid pools available for virus replication; possible effects on host membrane lipid balance not evaluated here
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Strong candidate for antiviral engineering in plants by elevating PAP activity to restrict membrane lipid availability for replication of some (+)RNA viruses
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Different effects on BMV/TMV versus TRV imply organelle- or virus-specific dependence on PAP-sensitive lipid environments

Pathway_Genes.Applied_Promoters: GAL1 promoter in yeast; enhanced CaMV 35S promoter in N. benthamiana
Pathway_Genes.Pathway_Branch_or_Subpathway: PA-to-DAG conversion controlling TAG versus phospholipid synthesis
Pathway_Genes.End_Product_Connection_Type: Competes with phospholipid synthesis by consuming PA and channeling flux toward storage lipid synthesis
Pathway_Genes.Rate_Limiting_or_Key_Control_Step: Key control step for lipid flux allocation
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Membrane structural lipids supporting viral replication complex formation
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: NbPAH1A inhibited BMV replication in yeast and more modestly in N. benthamiana
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. NbPAH1A expression in yeast inhibited BMV replication by approximately 40–50%. 2. In N. benthamiana, NbPAH1A expression inhibited BMV replication by about 25%. 3. NbPAH1A only slightly inhibited TMV replication and had no significant effect on TRV genomic RNAs 1 and 2.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Heterologous expression in yeast; transient overexpression in N. benthamiana by agroinfiltration; Northern blotting
Pathway_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Agroinfiltration-mediated overexpression followed by virus infection in N. benthamiana; galactose-induced expression in yeast
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Potential reprogramming of host phospholipid/storage lipid balance; antiviral efficacy appears weaker than PAH2-type orthologs
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Possible antiviral engineering target, but weaker effect suggests ortholog choice matters
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Paper notes different plant PAH orthologs may play different roles in plants

Pathway_Genes.Applied_Promoters: GAL1 promoter in yeast; enhanced CaMV 35S promoter in N. benthamiana
Pathway_Genes.Pathway_Branch_or_Subpathway: PA-to-DAG conversion controlling TAG versus phospholipid synthesis
Pathway_Genes.End_Product_Connection_Type: Competes with phospholipid synthesis by consuming PA and channeling flux toward storage lipid synthesis
Pathway_Genes.Rate_Limiting_or_Key_Control_Step: Key control step for lipid flux allocation
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Membrane structural lipids supporting viral replication complex formation
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: NbPAH2A inhibited BMV replication in yeast and strongly inhibited BMV and TMV replication in N. benthamiana
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. NbPAH2A expression in yeast inhibited BMV replication by approximately 40–50%. 2. In N. benthamiana, NbPAH2A expression inhibited BMV replication by about 40–50%. 3. NbPAH2A also inhibited TMV replication by about 50%, but did not significantly reduce TRV genomic RNAs 1 and 2.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Heterologous expression in yeast; transient overexpression in N. benthamiana by agroinfiltration; Northern blotting
Pathway_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Agroinfiltration-mediated overexpression followed by virus infection in N. benthamiana; galactose-induced expression in yeast
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: May alter host phospholipid allocation; broader antiviral effects may come with membrane lipid remodeling consequences
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Promising antiviral engineering candidate to restrict some membrane-dependent (+)RNA viruses in plants
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Stronger antiviral effect than NbPAH1A indicates functional divergence among N. benthamiana PAH homologs

Regulation_Genes.Regulation_Mode: Transcriptional repression relieved by sequestration under high PA conditions
Regulation_Genes.Regulatory_Effect_on_Target_Genes: Represses phospholipid synthesis genes; sequestration in pah1Δ context increases their transcription
Regulation_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Major membrane lipids required for membrane biogenesis and viral replication complexes
Regulation_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: High PA in pah1Δ is proposed to sequester Opi1p, increasing phospholipid synthesis gene expression and thereby favoring BMV replication
Regulation_Genes.Decisive_Influence_on_Target_Product: Condition-dependent transcriptional gate on phospholipid synthesis
Regulation_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. The paper states that increased PA in pah1Δ mutants elevates transcription of phospholipid synthesis genes through sequestration of Opi1p. 2. This regulatory framework was used to motivate CHO2 overexpression experiments.
Regulation_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Not directly tested in this study
Regulation_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: High-PA pah1Δ condition during BMV replication
Regulation_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Conceptually important for lipid-state regulation, but not directly validated here as an engineering target
Regulation_Genes.Feedback_or_Feedforward_Regulation: Links PA status to transcriptional control of phospholipid synthesis genes
Regulation_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Borderline core/background case; included because Opi1-mediated regulation is explicitly invoked in Results to explain the causal CHO2 experiment, though OPI1 itself was not directly manipulated
